request.question: What MTV television drama starred an actor who had once boxed with a boxer born in Belarus?

title: James Newman (actor)
Yuri Foreman
Jada (band)
Andrew Matarazzo
Ball Trap on the Cote Sauvage
Zay Harding
The Lyon's Den
Charlie Carver
Ron Mustafaa
Aly & AJ: Sister Act
sentences: James Milo Newman (born Jared Matthew James Newman; January 22, 1992) is an American actor.
He played Tony Schneider on the MTV television teen drama "Skins".
Newman's first acting experience was auditioning for "Skins".
He had previously planned to fight in the Golden Gloves boxing tournament (he had once boxed with Yuri Foreman).
Yuri Foreman (born August 5, 1980) is an Israeli professional boxer who held the WBA super welterweight title from 2009 to 2010.
He was born in Gomel, Belarus, but currently fights out of Brooklyn, New York.
Jada is an American pop and R&B female vocal group from Boston, Massachusetts.
They are known for their mix of pop, soul, dance, and R&B styling, and their gospel-inspired harmonies.
Jada was signed by Universal Motown Records president Sylvia Rhone in October 2006.
Jada won “Outstanding Pop/R&B Music Act of the Year” at the 2008 Boston Music Awards.
Jada is managed by Laura Poulin Management/ Bristol Entertainment.
Through Universal Motown, Jada released their first major label single, "American Cowboy" (written and produced by Akon and RedOne) in the spring of 2009.
This was followed by their second major label single, "Break Up Song" (written and produced by Wayne Wilkins, Andrew Frampton, and Savan Kotecha) in the fall of 2009.
Jada’s song "Model That" was featured on the November 17, 2009 episode of the MTV television show "The City".
"American Cowboy" was featured on the Fox drama "House" on Monday, April 27, 2009.
They released a song and music video for "This Party’s on Fire" (written and produced by Rio, video directed by Vassili Shields) on October 4, 2011.

Their most recent EP, named "Supersonique", was released on January 24, 2011, and included 5 songs with 2 remixes, including "Supersonique", "This Party's on Fire", "Mercy Killing", "Can't Let Go", and "Goodbye".
Andrew Matarazzo (born March 4, 1997) is a Los Angeles-based actor best known for joining the cast of the MTV television series "Teen Wolf" in its final season.

Ball Trap on the Cote Sauvage is a 1989 British television comedy drama (transmitted on BBC1), written by celebrated screenwriter Andrew Davies and directed by Jack Gold, set in France on Brittany's Côte Sauvage.
The one-off drama starred Jack Shepherd, Zoë Wanamaker, Michael Kitchen, Miranda Richardson and Erika Hoffman

Zavan Kerr "Zay" Harding (born November 1, 1974, Lihue, Hawaii) is an American television personality and actor, who may be best known as "Jeff Northcutt" on the MTV television serial, "Spyder Games", and as a host of the adventure travel television series, "Globe Trekker".
He is also the host of "Tough Trains", a travel series from the producers of Globe Trekker.
The Lyon's Den is a 2003 American legal drama television series set in Washington, D.C..

The legal drama starred Dennis "The Menace" Lyons as himself, newly appointed as partner of a long-established law firm that, as the plot revealed, harbored some dark secrets; the series' title and firm's name are allusions to the surname of Lowe, who also served as executive producer.

Much of the plot centered on the firm's internal politics and on Turner's attempts to uncover information on some of the firm's conspiracies while acting as the defense for some of the firm's higher-profile clients in a different case each episode.
Charlie Carver (born Charles Carver Martensen; July 31, 1988) is an American actor.

His best known roles include Porter Scavo on the ABC television series "Desperate Housewives", Ethan on the MTV television series "Teen Wolf", and Scott Frost on the first season of the HBO television series "The Leftovers".
His twin brother Max Carver has frequently portrayed the twin of his characters.
Imran Mahmood "Ron" Mustafaa (born December 3, 1989) is a young Bangladeshi Canadian actor.
He played Abbud Siddiqui on the MTV television teen drama "Skins".

Aly & AJ: Sister Act is an MTV television special featuring behind the scenes look of the lives of musicians/actresses Aly Michalka and her younger sister by two years, AJ Michalka, better known as Aly & AJ.

The special premiered on August 18, 2007, as part of a "My Super Sweet 16" marathon named "Aly & AJ's Super Sweet Playlist" hosted by the sisters, in which the sisters commented on their favorite episodes of the series.
It premiered with little advance publicity, but did have a segment of the Michalkas introducing the special, as well showcasing the MTV original television film "", which the Michalkas starred in.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What MTV television drama starred an actor who had once boxed with a boxer born in Belarus?
hop1_docs: James Newman (actor) | James Milo Newman (born Jared Matthew James Newman; January 22, 1992) is an American actor.  He played Tony Schneider on the MTV television teen drama "Skins".  Newman's first acting experience was auditioning for "Skins".  He had previously planned to fight in the Golden Gloves boxing tournament (he had once boxed with Yuri Foreman).

Leonardo DiCaprio filmography | Leonardo DiCaprio is an American actor and producer who started his career performing as a child on television.  He appeared on the shows "The New Lassie" (1989) and "Santa Barbara" (1990) and also had long running roles in the comedy-drama "Parenthood" (1990) and the sitcom "Growing Pains" (1991), before making his film debut in the 1991 direct-to-video release "Critters 3".  Two years later, he played Tobias Wolff opposite Robert De Niro in "This Boy's Life" (1993).  He followed this with a supporting role in "What's Eating Gilbert Grape" (1993), which earned him a nomination for the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor.  In 1995, DiCaprio played the American author Jim Carroll in "The Basketball Diaries" and the French poet Arthur Rimbaud in "Total Eclipse".  The following year he played Romeo Montague in the Baz Luhrmann-directed film "Romeo + Juliet" (1996).  DiCaprio starred opposite Kate Winslet in the James Cameron-directed film "Titanic" (1997).  The film became the highest grossing at the worldwide box-office, and made him famous globally.  For his performance, he received the MTV Movie Award for Best Male Performance and his first nomination for the Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Drama.

Richard Short (actor) | Richard Ian Porterfield Short (born 8 October 1975) is an English actor based in Los Angeles.  He is starring in the 2017 TV drama series "Mary Kills People".  In 2017 he appeared in the independent film "The Dare" and in 2016 "Crazy Famous".  On television he has had recurring roles on "Vinyl" and "Covert Affairs" and has appeared on "American Horror Story", "White Collar", and "Blue Bloods".  Short has appeared in more than 30 films and television shows in the USA and UK.  On Broadway, he was a member of the 2011 company of Jez Butterworth's Jerusalem at the Music Box Theatre.

Andres Hernández | Andres"Puppy"Hernandez (born in 1950) is a Puerto Rican former professional boxer who once fought Carlos Zarate for the World Boxing Council's world bantamweight title.  Particularly more so early in his career, he was known for being able to take many punches without falling; despite having four defeats by knockout, these came either after taking punishment for long periods against some of the hardest hitting boxers in history, or late in his career, when his ability had waned several levels.  His boxing nickname is "Puppy".

Matthew Davis | Matthew W. Davis (born May 8, 1978), also professionally known as Matt Davis, is an American actor who is known for his roles as Warner Huntington III in "Legally Blonde", Adam Hillman on the ABC comedy-drama "What About Brian" from 2006 to 2007 and Alaric Saltzman on The CW fantasy drama "The Vampire Diaries" from 2009 to 2017.  He starred on the short-lived CW mystery and horror drama "Cult" as Jeff Sefton, and had a recurring role on the CBS police drama "" as Sean Yeager.

Michael Jai White | Michael Jai White (born November 10, 1967) is an American actor and martial artist who has appeared in numerous films and television series.  He is the first African American to portray a major comic book superhero in a major motion picture, having starred as Al Simmons, the protagonist in the 1997 film "Spawn".  White appeared as Marcus Williams in the Tyler Perry films "Why Did I Get Married? " and "Why Did I Get Married Too? ", and starred as the character on the TBS/OWN comedy-drama television series "Tyler Perry's For Better or Worse".  White portrayed Jax Briggs in "".  White also portrayed boxer Mike Tyson in the 1995 HBO television movie "Tyson", and starred as Black Dynamite.

Shane West | Shane West (born Shannon Bruce Snaith; June 10, 1978) is an American actor, punk rock musician, and songwriter.  West is known for his portrayal of Eli Sammler in the ABC family drama "Once and Again", Landon Carter in "A Walk to Remember", Darby Crash in "What We Do Is Secret", Dr. Ray Barnett in the NBC medical drama "ER" and Michael Bishop in The CW spy drama "Nikita".  He also starred in the WGN fantasy/adventure/historical drama "Salem" as John Alden.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - James Newman — Passage [1] "James Newman (actor)" — "played Tony Schneider on the MTV television teen drama 'Skins'"; "he had once boxed with Yuri Foreman."
  - No passage here gives Yuri Foreman's page or birthplace.
  - Other passages list unrelated actors/roles (no other MTV drama + boxer links found).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Missing confirmation that the referenced boxer (Yuri Foreman) was born in Belarus.
  - No alternative actors in the corpus are shown to have boxed with a Belarus-born boxer.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Birthplace (country) of Yuri Foreman — specifically whether he was born in Belarus.

- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. Yuri Foreman — biography / Wikipedia page (confirm birthplace).
  2. Query: "Yuri Foreman birthplace" or "Yuri Foreman born in Belarus".
  3. If needed, corroborating source: "Yuri Foreman biography boxing" or reputable sports bio.
hop2_docs: Yuri Foreman | Yuri Foreman (born August 5, 1980) is an Israeli professional boxer who held the WBA super welterweight title from 2009 to 2010.  He was born in Gomel, Belarus, but currently fights out of Brooklyn, New York.

Miguel Cotto vs. Yuri Foreman | Miguel Cotto vs. Yuri Foreman, billed as Stadium Slugfest, was a boxing super welterweight fight for the WBA Super Welterweight championship.  The bout was held on June 5, 2010, at Yankee Stadium, in New York City, United States.  Cotto won the fight via technical knockout in the ninth round.

James Newman (actor) | James Milo Newman (born Jared Matthew James Newman; January 22, 1992) is an American actor.  He played Tony Schneider on the MTV television teen drama "Skins".  Newman's first acting experience was auditioning for "Skins".  He had previously planned to fight in the Golden Gloves boxing tournament (he had once boxed with Yuri Foreman).

Michael Kozlowski | Michael Kozlowski (born August 6, 1961) is a Russian-American boxing manager and trainer of Russian, Israeli, Panamanian, Chilean, and US champions, who handled the careers of Yuri Foreman (WBA World Champion), Jill Emery (IFBA World Champion), Luke Campbell (2012 Olympic Champion), Egor Plevako (European Champion), Roman Greenberg (1998 Cadet EUROPEAN silver medalist).  First trainer who brought Russian - Olympic boxing style to America.

George Foreman's KO Boxing | George Foreman's KO Boxing is a boxing video game produced by Acclaim, featuring boxer George Foreman, released in 1992.  Years later, Acclaim released another game featuring Foreman, "Foreman For Real".

Foreman School District | Foreman School District is a public school district based in Foreman, Arkansas, United States.  The Foreman School District provides early childhood, elementary and secondary education for more than 500 kindergarten through grade 12 students at its five facilities.  Foreman School District is accredited by the Arkansas Department of Education (ADE).

George Foreman Grill | The George Foreman Lean Mean Fat-Reducing Grilling Machine, commonly known as the George Foreman Grill, is an indoor, electrically heated grill manufactured by Spectrum Brands.  It is promoted by former boxing champion George Foreman.  Since its introduction in 1994, over 100 million George Foreman grills have been sold worldwide.
response.summary_2: Skins. James Newman played Tony Schneider on MTV's Skins and had once boxed with Yuri Foreman, who was born in Gomel, Belarus.
